quote: The biggest thing would be a cost savings, but I gotta get by the cost of the solar panels first.
stewart_read: James embeds a cost objection directly inside his answer to the interest-in-solar question — the verify section's highest-depth item. Tyke's 'Mhmm. Yeah. That makes sense.' is a capture-and-drop: the data is acknowledged, the emotional tension is not engaged. The flip was available here: the cost James is worried about is the bill he's already paying, not the solar system. Per objection_inversion, the move is to redirect the cost frame — 'what you can't get by is the bill you're already paying' — not to validate the barrier and move on. This is a hidden_costs_anxiety signal that the codex flags as TBD in the rebuttals section.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Tyke is deferring the cost conversation to the bill-collection section where he'll have a concrete number to anchor against. Rejected — Tyke never returns to the cost objection even after capturing the $350/month bill at 02:38.
coaching_implication: Show Tyke the two moments back-to-back: 00:42 (cost objection) and 02:38 (bill capture). Ask him: 'You had the number at 02:38 — what was the move?' The goal is to build the habit of reconnecting the bill to the cost fear James named explicitly. No fabricated savings numbers needed — the flip is emotional, not mathematical.

quote: No. But I think the the if this works out Mhmm. You're gonna go put them up on my house on the Eastern Plains. I have a home out there that I've built, like, seven years ago and never lived in it. But I raised, dairy cows out there, and it it would definitely be something I'd be interested out there as well.
stewart_read: James volunteers a second qualifying property — a seven-year-old home on the Eastern Plains used for a dairy operation — entirely unprompted. This is a high-value enthusiasm signal: a second address, a commercial-adjacent load profile (dairy operation), and a customer who is already mentally extending the relationship beyond the current call. Tyke's response ('let's start with this home first') is a reasonable triage move for call management but leaves the Eastern Plains property with no routing, no capture of the address, and no pipeline structure. Note: this signal falls into the TBD gap in the cross_sell_signals catalog — there is no existing codex entry for second-property solar installs or agricultural/commercial-adjacent loads. The analysis_directives.cross_sell_signal_detection directive applies, but the specific routing protocol for a second residential/agricultural property is not yet defined in the codex.
alternative_hypothesis: Tyke's 'let's start with this home first' is a strategic deferral — he intends to return to the Eastern Plains property after qualifying the Denver home. Rejected — the Eastern Plains property is never mentioned again, and the call ends without any capture of the second address or routing instruction.
coaching_implication: The Eastern Plains property should have been captured as a separate lead in real time: address, utility, rough usage context (dairy operation = elevated load). Even if Tyke couldn't work both properties on one call, the correct move is 'I'm going to have someone reach out separately about the Eastern Plains home — can I get that address?' The manager should use this moment to establish the floor's protocol for second-property disclosures.

quote: If I average it through the year, probably $3.50.
stewart_read: Tyke captures the bill amount and follows up on trend ('Has it kind of always been that high?'). The follow-up is a good instinct — it surfaces the granddaughter context and humanizes the bill. But the bill is captured and dropped: it is never flipped into emotional alignment against the utility, and it is never reconnected to the cost objection James raised at 00:42. Per bill_anchoring, capture alone is necessary but not sufficient — the flip is the move. Note on the transcript figure: '$3.50' is almost certainly a phonetic rendering of '$350' given the context (Accel utility, Denver home, granddaughter running heat). The $350/month interpretation is strongly supported by context, but the transcript is ambiguous. The coaching note should flag this — if Tyke heard '$3.50' and didn't clarify, that's a separate gap; if the transcript is a transcription artifact, the actual number should be confirmed from the recording.
coaching_implication: Two things to address: (1) Confirm the actual bill figure from the recording — '$3.50' vs '$350' is a transcription ambiguity that matters for pipeline notes. (2) Show Tyke the gap between capturing the bill and flipping it. The granddaughter detail he surfaced at 02:54 was the emotional hook — 'she gets cold and turns it up' is the bill pain made human. That's the moment to say: 'So your bill is partly driven by keeping your granddaughter comfortable — solar locks that cost in instead of letting it keep climbing.' That's the flip. He had the material and didn't use it.

quote: You know what? It will be, sir, because, before this divorce I had a a A 760 credit score. And right now, it's set in just about, 625.
stewart_read: James discloses a divorce, a credit score drop from 760 to 625, and inherited debt — a significant vulnerability disclosure. Tyke's response ('Yeah. All her debt, all her taxes, and everything else. Yeah. I'm sorry to hear about that.') shows surface-level empathy: he mirrors the content and offers a brief apology. What's missing is the bridge to value and the pipeline-preservation move. The 760 baseline is critical information — it tells Tyke this is a temporarily disqualified customer with a strong credit history, not a structural DQ. Tyke's exit ('I'll take you off the list for now') discards that signal. The correct move is to acknowledge the situation, validate the 760 baseline as the real James, and structure the six-month callback James himself proposed.
alternative_hypothesis: Tyke's clean exit is strategic — he's respecting James's autonomy and not pressuring a customer in a difficult life situation. Partially valid, but the strategic read breaks down at 'I'll take you off the list for now' — that's a pipeline disposal, not a respectful exit. A respectful exit with pipeline preservation would lock the callback James proposed.
coaching_implication: The manager should distinguish between the empathy Tyke showed (real, appropriate) and the pipeline move he missed (locking the callback). The coaching question is not 'why didn't you empathize more' — it's 'James told you he'd be back in six months and gave you a 760 baseline. What did you do with that?' The answer should be a structured callback with a date, not 'I'll take you off the list.'

quote: I'll be looking for you in about six months after I get all this crap she that I inherited from her.
stewart_read: James self-proposes a six-month return window with a specific rationale. This is a callback WITH a locked rationale — not a brush-off. Tyke's response is 'I'll take you off the list for now' — a clean exit that converts a self-proposed pipeline signal into a cold lead. Per soft_outcome_distinction, a callback with a locked time and rationale is materially different from 'we'll touch base.' Tyke had the opportunity to say: 'Six months — I'll put that in my calendar. Can I reach you at this number in [month]?' That converts a warm exit into a structured pipeline entry.
alternative_hypothesis: Tyke's exit is appropriate given the hard DQ on credit — booking a callback on a disqualified lead wastes floor resources. Rejected — James's 760 baseline and self-proposed timeline make this a temporarily disqualified lead, not a structural DQ. The comp model (paid on sits) makes pipeline preservation directly relevant to Tyke's paycheck.
coaching_implication: Show Tyke the difference between 'off the list' and 'on the calendar for six months.' The specific move: confirm the month, confirm the phone number, and tell James you'll reach out then. This takes 20 seconds and converts a warm exit into a recoverable pipeline entry. Given the stair-step comp model, a sit six months from now is still a sit.

quote: Nope. It should just be me. K. And one young man told me on the last phone call for Windows that, my I even hate to say it. My ex wife, she was still she was still coming up, and I'm gonna make sure that that's taken care of as well. But there's nobody responsible other than me.
stewart_read: James discloses that his ex-wife may still appear on title records — a live title ambiguity that could affect the appointment and the close. Tyke's response is 'Okay. No. That's perfect. I'll get that notated down then.' — he captures the note and moves on. The title question is not resolved; it's deferred. This is not a spouse_decision protocol violation (James is not asking to consult a spouse), but it is an unresolved title ambiguity that would surface at the closer stage. The notation is the right move; the gap is that Tyke doesn't flag this as a potential DQ risk or ask a clarifying question about the timeline for resolution.
alternative_hypothesis: Tyke's notation is sufficient — the closer handles title verification, not the setter. Partially valid, but given that the call ends in a DQ on credit, the title ambiguity becomes a handoff note that never gets used. If the call had proceeded to a booking, this would be a critical handoff item.
coaching_implication: Use this moment to reinforce the handoff brief discipline: title ambiguities belong in the closer's brief, not just in a CRM note. The specific question Tyke could have asked: 'Do you know roughly when that'll be cleared up?' — not to resolve it, but to give the closer a timeline.

quote: I do see your your neighbor also has solar, so that's a good sign.
stewart_read: Tyke uses satellite imagery to surface a neighbor-has-solar signal and deploys it as a legitimacy/social-proof moment. This is a competent micro-move — it's personalized, it's grounded in the specific address, and it reinforces that the home is a viable candidate. It's not a flip or a curiosity-creation move, but it does build mild forward momentum at a point in the call where James is still in verify mode.
coaching_implication: This is a positive moment worth naming in the 1-on-1 — Tyke is using the satellite pull actively, not just as a data-collection step. The coaching question is whether he can extend this instinct to the bill flip: he's good at using external data (neighbor's solar) to build confidence; the same instinct applied to James's own bill number would be the flip.
